metaTitle: Features — Prosperity | Loot Overhaul for Minecraft
metaDescription: Every Prosperity feature: per-player instanced loot, unlooted container indicators, five distance-scaled loot tiers, structure overrides, the loot modifier API, and datapack loot injection.
ogDescription: Detailed breakdown of every Prosperity feature and mechanic.
title: Features
lede: Everything Prosperity does to make loot fair and worth discovering. Most systems are individually toggleable via config.

html: Every naturally generated container serves each player their own independent loot the first time they open it. The vanilla block, model, block entity, and renderer are never touched — Prosperity attaches per-player loot to vanilla containers via persistent Fabric data attachments, intercepting interactions through events.
After the first generation, the container's vanilla loot table is nullified to prevent hopper and comparator exploits. This replaces the functionality of Lootr.

items.title: Per-Player Loot
html: First visit rolls fresh loot from the container's loot table and the player's context (luck, position), stored under their UUID. Everyone who opens the chest gets their own copy.

items.title: Hoppers & Comparators
html: Hoppers see an empty container and cannot extract instanced loot; comparators read zero. This is intended — instanced loot lives outside the vanilla inventory.

items.title: Container Coverage
html: Chests, trapped chests, barrels, shulker boxes, and loot dispensers/droppers are all instanced, plus chest and hopper minecarts — so mineshaft loot, which is overwhelmingly minecarts, is first-class. Each player opens their own instance of a cart just as they do a chest.

Containers you have never opened display a small gold sparkle sprite hovering above them; containers you have looted show nothing. No more re-checking every chest in a stronghold or mansion. Container minecarts carry the same sparkle, anchored to the cart so it follows along as the minecart moves.
The indicator is a world-space overlay (not a HUD element), driven client-side from lightweight per-chunk requests as chunks load.
Loot quality scales with Euclidean distance from world origin (0, 0 in the XZ plane). Origin is used rather than world spawn so distance reflects absolute geography, not a movable reference.

blocks.note: Stack quantities scale by the multiplier (capped at 64); quality adds luck to the loot context. The End is always treated as Depths tier.

html: The same tiers apply to hostile-mob drops when you land the killing blow, so combat keeps pace with exploration — a mob far from origin drops scaled stacks and rarer loot, while passive mobs and environmental or non-player kills are untouched. Mob scaling is independently togglable.

Override the tier used by a specific structure with a fixed, minimum, or maximum mode — e.g. ocean monuments fixed to Wilderness, strongholds at minimum Outlands, or village chests capped at Frontier.

items.title: Loot Modifier API
html: A Fabric-style LootModifierCallback.EVENT fires after distance scaling but before the loot table resolves, handing listeners a mutable context. See the API page.

items.title: Loot Table Injection
html: A datapack system adds custom items to vanilla loot tables gated by minimum distance tier, and optionally by dimension. Prosperity ships built-in injections so distance scaling feels meaningful out of the box.

items.title: Loot Tier Feedback
html: Opening an instanced container shows an action-bar message with the loot tier and active modifiers, so the risk-versus-reward loop of exploration is visible.

items.title: Tier HUD Badge
html: A compact badge in the shared rfizzle HUD strip (slot 3) shows your current distance loot tier — a treasure-chest glyph and the tier name, drawn in the tier's color, updating live as you travel. Crossing a tier boundary briefly flashes the label gold. It stacks below Tribulation and Mercantile when installed, honors a configurable corner and offset, and is independently togglable.

items.title: Loot Refresh
html: Optional restocking (off by default) lets a container's loot regenerate per player after a configurable cooldown, so explored structures stay worth revisiting on long-running worlds. Each player's cooldown is independent; once it elapses the unlooted sparkle reappears and the next open rolls fresh loot.

items.title: Anti-Grief Break Resistance
html: Optional protection (off by default) makes a loot container that some online player still has pending break several times slower, with a quiet cue when breaking begins — so one player cannot quickly destroy a dungeon's chests and erase everyone else's instanced loot. It is a speed bump, not a wall: creative players break instantly, and protection lifts once every online player has opened the container. Player-placed chests are never affected.
